theory.concept:
A very common interview pattern: instead of the overall top N rows, you need the top N rows within each group — e.g. the top 2 highest scorers per city.

- GROUP BY alone can't do this since it collapses rows; instead, use a ranking window function (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, or DENSE_RANK) partitioned by the group, then filter on that rank
- Since window functions can't be filtered directly in the same clause they're computed in, wrap the ranked query in a CTE or derived table, and filter in the outer query
- ROW_NUMBER always gives exactly N rows per group even with ties; RANK can return more than N rows if there's a tie at the Nth position — choose based on whether ties should count as sharing a position or not

shared_table.name: Students
shared_table.create_sql:
CREATE TABLE Students (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT,
  age INTEGER,
  city TEXT,
  marks INTEGER
);
shared_table.insert_sql:
INSERT INTO Students (id, name, age, city, marks) VALUES
(1, 'Aditya', 21, 'Kolkata', 78),
(2, 'Priya', 22, 'Delhi', 85),
(3, 'Rohan', 21, 'Kolkata', 62),

questions.prompt: Find the top scorer in each city.
Find the top 2 scorers in each city, only for cities with at least 2 students.
Find the second-highest scorer in each city, only for cities with at least 2 students.
